did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning approach, specifically a novel optimized Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture named DSSCC-Net, integrated with SMOTE-Tomek oversampling for class imbalance handling. This AI model was applied to dermoscopic images from the HAM10000, ISIC 2018, and PH2 datasets to perform skin cancer classification (diagnosis of 7 skin lesion types including melanoma). Results were obtained using metrics such as accuracy (97.82%), precision, recall, and AUC, along with Grad-CAM for interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel deep learning framework (DSSCC-Net) integrated with SMOTE-Tomek for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods (CNN architecture, data augmentation, SMOTE-Tomek), experiments conducted on three benchmark datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2018, PH2), and reports new empirical results including accuracy (97.82%), precision, recall, and AUC metrics, along with statistical significance testing. While it includes a related work section, its primary purpose is reporting new technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: DSSCC net enhanced skin cancer classification using SMOTE Tomek and optimized convolutional neural network
brief_summary: This study proposes DSSCC-Net, a novel optimized Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture integrated with SMOTE-Tomek oversampling to address class imbalance in skin cancer classification. The model was trained and validated on three benchmark datasets: HAM10000, ISIC 2018, and PH2, focusing on seven skin lesion types including melanoma. DSSCC-Net achieved an average accuracy of 97.82%, precision of 97%, recall of 97%, and an AUC of 99.43%, outperforming state-of-the-art models like VGG-16 and ResNet-152. The integration of SMOTE-Tomek significantly improved minority-class detection, while Grad-CAM analysis confirmed the model's high interpretability and spatial alignment with expert-labeled masks. The results demonstrate that DSSCC-Net offers robust performance and deployment readiness for clinical integration in dermatology.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (AKIC, BCC, BKL, DF, NV, VASC, MEL)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin cancer types using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) from the HAM10000, ISIC 2018, and PH2 datasets. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy (97.82%), precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC. The task involves distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions as well as specific lesion types.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A widely used collection of dermoscopic images containing 10,015 images of seven different skin lesion classes. Images were collected from diverse populations using various modalities. Over half of the cases are supported by histopathological examination, while the rest are supported by follow-up examination, expert opinion, or in vivo confocal microscopy.

HAM10000.metric: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, AUC, Loss, Model Size, Inference Time
HAM10000.notes: DSSCC-Net achieved 97.82% ± 0.37% average accuracy across 5-fold cross-validation. Specificity was 96.9%. With SMOTE-Tomek, AUC was 99.43%; without, it was 96.65%. Dice coefficient was 83.0% and IoU was 71.7% for explainability validation against expert masks. Confusion matrix counts: AKIEC TP=1302, BCC TP=1382, BKL TP=1297, DF TP=1331, NV TP=1217, VASC TP=1303, MEL TP=1347.

Resolution_Ablation_HAM10000.notes: Increasing resolution beyond 28x28 yielded marginal accuracy gains (+1.1% max) but increased computational cost by 3-5 times. 28x28 was selected as default for edge deployment.

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 28x28 pixels (with ablation studies at 64x64 and 128x128)
SMOTE-Tomek oversampling (applied post-split to address class imbalance)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Synthetic minority oversampling technique applied post-split (after train/val/test split) to address class imbalance without causing data leakage. SMOTE generates synthetic samples for minority classes using KNN interpolation, while Tomek links clean the dataset by removing instances near the decision surface. Compared against MixUp, CutMix, and GAN-based augmentation.
Mentioned as employed to enhance feature extraction and reduce overfitting, but specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation, flip, zoom) are not detailed in the provided text.

conclusions: The proposed DSSCC-Net model, an optimized Convolutional Neural Network integrated with SMOTE-Tomek oversampling, achieved state-of-the-art performance in skin cancer classification, demonstrating robustness against class imbalance.

DSSCC-Net achieved an average accuracy of 97.82% ± 0.37%, precision of 97%, recall of 97%, and an AUC of 99.43% across the HAM10000, ISIC 2018, and PH2 datasets, outperforming baseline models such as VGG-16, ResNet-152, and EfficientNet-B0.
The integration of SMOTE-Tomek significantly improved minority-class detection, increasing the AUC from 96.65% (without SMOTE-Tomek) to 99.43% (with SMOTE-Tomek).
Statistical significance testing (paired t-tests and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests) confirmed that the performance improvements of DSSCC-Net over baseline models were significant (p < 0.05).

Explainability analysis using Grad-CAM, validated against expert-labeled masks with a Dice coefficient of 83.0% and IoU of 71.7%, confirmed the model's high interpretability and spatial alignment with expert annotations.

Ablation studies on input resolution demonstrated that a 28x28 pixel resolution provided the best trade-off for edge-device deployment, offering high accuracy (98.0%) with minimal computational cost (3.42 MB model size, 12.6 ms inference time) compared to higher resolutions which yielded only marginal accuracy gains (+1.1%) but significantly increased computational load.

The study highlights that current CNN-based models often struggle with underrepresented skin lesion classes due to dataset imbalances, and DSSCC-Net addresses this by effectively handling class imbalance without data leakage through post-split oversampling.
DSSCC-Net demonstrates readiness for real-world clinical integration and deployment on low-resource/edge devices due to its compact architecture and efficient inference speed.
Future work will focus on extending the DSSCC-Net framework to other medical imaging domains, other cancer types, and developing real-time diagnostic applications in telemedicine.
important_remarks: The study addresses class imbalance in skin cancer datasets (specifically the dominance of the 'nv' class in HAM10000) by using SMOTE-Tomek oversampling applied strictly after the train/validation/test split to prevent data leakage.

The model was trained on dermoscopic images resized to 28x28 pixels; ablation studies showed that increasing resolution to 64x64 or 128x128 yielded only marginal accuracy gains (+1.1%) but increased computational cost by 3-5 times, favoring the smaller input for edge deployment.

Performance was evaluated on three benchmark datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2018, PH2), but the paper does not report detailed per-class metrics (precision, recall, F1) for ISIC 2018 and PH2, limiting the assessment of class-specific performance on these external datasets.

The HAM10000 dataset contains images from diverse populations and modalities, with over half supported by histopathology, but the remaining cases rely on follow-up, expert opinion, or confocal microscopy, which may introduce label noise or variability.

Interpretability was validated using Grad-CAM against expert-labeled masks, achieving a mean Dice coefficient of 83.0% and IoU of 71.7%, indicating strong spatial alignment between model attention and clinical lesion regions.

The model achieved a melanoma recall of 97%, which is clinically significant for minimizing false negatives in screening, though specific false positive rates for melanoma were not explicitly detailed in the provided context.

Cross-dataset validation showed high performance when training on HAM10000 and testing on ISIC 2018 (96.7% accuracy) and PH2 (95.5% accuracy), but performance dropped when training on PH2 (small dataset, n=200) and testing on HAM10000 (94.7% accuracy), suggesting potential sensitivity to dataset size and distribution shifts.

The paper claims state-of-the-art performance but compares against a mix of standard CNNs and some less common or older variants (e.g., Enhanced VGG-19, Vgg-19 V2), and does not include comparison with the most recent transformer-based models beyond Swin Transformer and ConvNeXt.
